title: Linux Firewall Configuration
description: Configure host-based firewalls using ufw, firewalld, and iptables to control inbound and outbound network traffic.

tasks.prompt: Display the current status and rules of the ufw firewall.
tasks.explanation: 'ufw status' shows whether the firewall is active and lists configured allow/deny rules.

tasks.prompt: Enable the ufw firewall (will prompt for confirmation on remote SSH sessions).
tasks.explanation: 'ufw enable' activates the firewall and configures it to start on boot.

tasks.prompt: Allow incoming SSH connections (port 22/tcp) through ufw.
tasks.hint: Use ufw allow ssh: 'sudo ufw allow ssh'
tasks.explanation: 'ufw allow ssh' adds a rule permitting inbound TCP traffic on port 22 using the built-in SSH profile.

tasks.prompt: Allow incoming TCP traffic on port 443 through ufw.
tasks.hint: Use ufw allow with port: 'sudo ufw allow 443/tcp'
tasks.explanation: 'ufw allow 443/tcp' permits HTTPS traffic, commonly needed for web servers.

tasks.prompt: Deny all incoming traffic from IP address '192.168.1.100' via ufw.
tasks.hint: Use ufw deny from: 'sudo ufw deny from 192.168.1.100'
tasks.explanation: 'ufw deny from' blocks all inbound connections originating from the specified source IP.

tasks.prompt: List all rules in the iptables filter table with line numbers.
tasks.explanation: 'iptables -L -n --line-numbers' shows filter table rules numerically without DNS resolution.

tasks.prompt: Allow incoming TCP traffic on port 80 in iptables INPUT chain.
tasks.hint: Use iptables append rule: 'sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT'
tasks.explanation: This appends (-A) a rule to INPUT allowing TCP destination port 80 with action ACCEPT.

tasks.prompt: Set the default policy of the iptables INPUT chain to DROP.
tasks.hint: Use iptables policy: 'sudo iptables -P INPUT DROP'
tasks.explanation: 'iptables -P' sets the default policy for a chain. DROP silently discards unmatched packets.

tasks.prompt: Check whether firewalld is running on this RHEL-style system.
tasks.explanation: 'firewall-cmd --state' reports if firewalld is running, the default firewall on RHEL/CentOS/Fedora.

tasks.prompt: Permanently allow the 'http' service through firewalld.
tasks.explanation: '--permanent' saves the rule across reboots. '--add-service=http' opens the predefined HTTP ports.
